text: Howard, 10 C.B., 290, followed. The lease contained a covenant by the lessee to pay "'such further sums as rent as shall represent one third of the annual sum payable... from time to time during the said term by the said lessor... as land tax to the Commissioners of Taxation under the Land ani Income Taxx Assessment Act 1895, or any amendment thereof in respect of the land demised." Subse. quently to the execution of the lease, the Land Tax (Leases) Act, 1902, was passed. Held, that the covenant was void.
citation: Harris v Sydney Glass and Tile Co [1904] HCA 47

text: Per Grifith C.J., and Barton J. :—The intention of the parties, as disclosed by the lease, was that the stipulation in the covenant should be in substi tion for the statutory provision then in force, and for any statutory provision that might thereafter be made in place of it, and was therefore void under the Lani and Income Assessment Act 1895, and inoperative under the Land Tar (Zease) Act 1902; and furcher, that, even if the parties intended it be cumulative, it was void under the Land and Income Tax Assessment Act 1895 and that the original quality of sterility imposed by that upon the stipulation wien it 'was mace still attached to it. Per O'Connor J. :—The stipulation, being a violation of the provisions of sec. 63 of the Land and Income Assessment Act 1895 was void from its inc tion, and therefore, whatever the effect of the stipulation might have been, ifit had been made after the passing of the Land Tax (Leases) Act 1902, it could not be made effectual for the purpose of making good the claim for rent alleged to have accrued due under it after the commencement of the latter Act. When a party has obtained a new trial on the ground of the impropet rejection of evidence, if the evidence was relevant to the issue on which it 'was tendered and the ground on which the evidence was originally rejected was erroneous, itis not a ground for allowing an appeal from the order grant{ng the new trial, that the exact grounds of relevancy were not stated to the Court, Dev n of the Supreme Court, 4 S.R., 454, affirmed.
citation: Harris v Sydney Glass and Tile Co [1904] HCA 47

text: OF AUSTRALIA. 20LR.) ales. : ba was an action brought by the appellant against the ponents to recover £94 15s. 2d, rent due under a lease hereby the appellant let certain premises to the respondents for 'Hem of years. 'The reddendum of the lease, which was under "amass follows: " Yielding and paying therefor yearly and very year during the said term . . . the rent or sum of {00 sterling money by several equal payments of £75 each athe first days of April, July, October, and January in each yarnd also yielding and paying therefor yearly and every year upon demand such further sums as rent as shalll represent one"third of the annual sum payable from time to time during the "sid term by the said lessor . . . as land tax to the Commissiners of Taxation under the Land and Income Tax Assessment "de 1895, or any amendment thereof in respect of the land The lessee covenanted to pay the rent or rents reserved on the ays appointed by and under the lease "without any deduction orabatement whatsoever." 'The respondents' fourth plea was a plea on equitable grounds, | i8to £75 of the amount claimed, to the effect that they had tented the lease on the faith of an agreement made prior to the date of the lease, by which the appellant had agreed to allow them 50 per cent, off the rent for the first six months, and had 'tuslly allowed that amount and accepted the balance in full _ Stisfction and discharge of the first six months' rent. The effect of the plea is more fully set out in the judgment Uklivered by Grigith C.J. There was also a fifth plea, as to £19 15s. 2d., the amount -thined under the covenant to pay as rent one-third of the tountof land tax for each year, that that portion of the reddenmwas void under sec, 63 of the Land and Income Tas: Assessment Act 1895.

text: At the trial it appeared that the respondents, on 16th November MW, wrote a letter to the appellant setting out the conditions on pote were willing to accept the offer to lease, and stating a 16 "ona fom a decision of the Supreme Court of New South H.©. or A. 1904, 'Tix Co. 230 HIGH COURT H.C.orA. inter alia, "our lease is to commence from Ist January, 1004 und you agree to allow us 50 per cent. off the rent for the fy Haus six months." 'The solicitor for the appellant replied to this lt Y sroxer Suggesting certain modifications on other points, but not qualify. Giass aso ing this particular proposal, and stating further, "otherwise the NE © terms of your letter are satisfactory." 'The respondents repli assenting to the proposed modifications, and the lease yas executed, These letters were tendered in evidence in support the fourth plea, and rejected, under circumstances appearing more fully in the judgments. Darley C.J., who presided at the trial, held that the covenant dealing with the additional rent was not an infringement of sec. 63 of the Land and Income Tur Assess ment Act 1895, and directed a verdict for the plaintiff, appellant in this appeal, for £94 15s. 2d. The defendants, respondents in this appeal, appealed to the Full Court, who granted a rule absolute for a new trial as to £75, and ordered that judgment be entered for the defendants as to the £19 15s. 2d.

text: The fact that the words "as rent" are used, and that the covenant is placed in the reddendum, cannot affect the nature of the covenant. Sec. 12 directs that the whole tax shall be paid in the first instance by the land lord, and that he may then recover contribution in certain specified proportions. Therefore any arrangement which hasiafact altered, or might have the effect of altering, those proportios affects the incidence of the tax and is forbidden by sec. 63.

This is an appeal from a decision of the Supreme Court of New South Wales making absolute an order to enter judgment for the defendants as to £19 15s. 2d., and 'manew trial as to £75, the residue of the plaintiff's claim. __ Thequestions raised as to the two amounts are entirely different. _The action was brought on a covenant in a lease of land demised 'TW the plaintiff to the defendants for a term of fifty years, from 2rd December. (3) 1 K.B., 531, (4) 17 DLR. 110.

text: HIGH COURT (9 Ast January, 1902, at a rental of £300 a year, together with "such further sums as rent as shall represent one-third of the annual sum payable from time to time during the said term by the said lessor . . as land tax to the Commissioners of Taxa. tion under the Land and Income Tax Assessment Act 1895, or any amendment thereof in respect of the land demised." The action was brought to recover two years' additional rent under the words Ihave just read, and also £75, being half the first year's rent of £150. The question with regard to the £75 arose upon the defendants' fourth plea, pleaded upon equitable grounds 'That plea was to the following eftect :—As to £75 part of the rent claimed by the plaintiff, that before they executed the deed it was agreed between the plaintiff and the defendants that the plaintiff should allow the defendants 50 per cent. off the rent for the first six months of the term of the lease, and that the defendants executed the said deed upon the faith of the said agreement, and not otherwise, and that the plaintiff duly, and in accordance with the terms of the said agreement, allowed the defendants 50 per cent. off the rent so covenanted to be paid, and reserved by and in the said deed, for the first six months of the said term, and that the defendants duly paid the remainder of the said rent due under the said covenant, and the plaintiff accepted the same in satisfaction and discharge of the whole of the said rent, and that the plaintiff is now suing for the said rent as agreed to be allowed _ off to the defendants, being the said £75, in fraud of the agreement. At the trial, before the learned Chief Justice, certain letters were tendered in evidence to prove the allegations contained in : that plea, but he rejected the evidence, holding that it was intended to qualify an agreement under seal, and was therefore nadmissible.
citation: Harris v Sydney Glass and Tile Co [1904] HCA 47

'The result of the appeal was, s myonity of the Court being of opinion that the evidence tothe £75 was admissible, that it was directed that there vw iubea new trial as to that sum, and that as to the £19 15s. 'extra rent a verdict should be entered for the defendants. there is no doubt about the general rule as to the inadmisilty of written evidence or oral evidence of statements by parties to qualify the terms of a deed. It is stated very aly by James Li, in Leggott v. Barrett (1).

The foundation of his judgment was that the agree. ment alleged was upona subject not dealt with by the lease The agreement as to the £75 does in one sense tend to qualify terms of the lease, in that the result will be that, instead of $3 a year (which is the amount reserved by the lease) being paid ll through the term, rent will be paid at the rate of only £150 per annum for the first six months. text: The point is one of great difficulty, but, in the view which we take of the case, it is not necessary to decide whether this objection is valid or not Because, although the plea is called an equitable one, it may allege facts which would afford a good defence to that part of the claim to which it is pleaded, and the defendants were entitled to take advantage of it in any sense in which it can be read. It is not merely a plea setting up the making of the agreement, and alleging that the plaintiff is suing in breach — of it, but it also alleges that the agreement was performed, that there had been a balanee struck, and payment of that: balance. It will be convenient first to consider the plea as if it set up am agreement, having nothing to do with the lease but relating to entirely extrinsic matter, by which the plaintiff had agreed to Pay the defendants £75, or to allow it to them on a settlement of accounts.
citation: Harris v Sydney Glass and Tile Co [1904] HCA 47

text: From that point of view the plea would operate asa plea of payment, or perhaps of accord and satisfaction. For that Proposition the case of Cullander v. Howard (2) is clearly a authority. 'The plea there was that, "after the accruing of a causes of action, &., and before the commencement of the sit, &e., the defendant and the plaintiff accounted together, and am (1) LR. 10 QB. 174, (2) 10 C.B., 290, ' { OF AUSTRALIA. 239 'aus then stated between them, of and concerning the Hor a. certain other claims, &., and pa the sum of £50 was then found — annus unt 'on that accounting whe and then was, due and owing from the defendant to the ssigy 'ghintif; which sum of money the defendant then, in consideraGuass axp fon of the premises, promised the plaintiff to pay him on request 'that thereupon the defendant afterwards, and before the comnt of this suit, &e., paid to the plaintiff, &e,, the sum of ss) in full satisfaction and discharge of such last mentioned sum." this plea there was a special demurrer, and a joinder in ume. The Court in giving judgment for the defendant, Grifith C9. on the plea as an informal plea of mit, and stated the doctrine governing the matter. Wilde {J sid(1): "If the plea in this case amounts to an alleganof the allowance of cross demands upon an account stated, yayment of the balance, there seems to be no doubt but that "iiets up substantially a good defence to the action. 'This appears "iplavebeen established, and the reason of the doetrine expounded, "Ti the obligor or feoffor be bound by condition to pay one marks at a certain day, and at the day the parties do tothe obligor or feoffor, that sum is allowed, and the residue of hundred marks paid, this is a good satisfaction; and yet the was a chose-in-action. and no payment was made thereof, but -lpway of retainer or discharge.' The authority cited for this ge, is a ease to that effect in the Year Book, 11 Ric.

The admitted debt having been satisfied, the validity of the contract under which it was paid is not material, when the whole matter has been settled on the basis that the contract was a good one which imposed an obligation on the parties, whether perfect or imperfect. 'The circumstance that the invalidity of the alleged contract depends upon its contra. dieting a deed, whether the deed sued on or another, cannot make a difference in principle. The plea in this case, whether the alleged agreement was valid or invalid, sets up such a defence, and is in effect a plea of payment.

text: Therefore, if it is proved, the action fails. It is not necessary to refer in detail to the documents tendered in evidence on this point. text: It was contended that, if the evidence was tendered on an — erroneous ground, we should refuse to grant a new trial. But the position is this: the defendants have obtained an order for a new trial on the ground that certain evidence was rejected whieh was admissible. I express no opinion whether the alleged agreement was valid or invalid: in either case the evidence was adinissible, as tending to prove the plea. The snbstantial ground on which it was tendered was that it tended to prove the plea, 'The substantial ground of rejection was that the plea was a bad one, ie., that the alleged agreement, although executed, afforded no defence to the action.
citation: Harris v Sydney Glass and Tile Co [1904] HCA 47

text: The ground on which the evidence was tendered was therefore right, and it was the objection which was illfounded. We do not think that the mere rejection of evidence on an erroneous ground prejudices the right of the party who tenders it to a new trial. So far as the £75 is concerned, the appeal therefore fails, In this judgment we all concur.

text: We must first ascertain what it is to which the parties have agreed, and then inquire whether that agreement is or is not valid, having regard to the express provisions of the law. Leaving, then, the provisions of sec. 63 out of consideration for a moment, i.e. assuming that the incidence of the land tax may be altered by contract, does this stipulation, fairly construed, alter it ? It is contended for the defendants that, wpon the true construe tion of the lease, the intention was that the payment of a sum under the name of rent, equal to one-third of the land tax forthe — time being, was to be in lieu of and to supersede the tenants obligation to contribute the proportion prescribed by sec. 12 of the Act.

And the legislature must, therefore, be taken tp have meant that the parties should not be able to provide by agreement that one of them should eseape an additional bundey_ which might be east upon him by a future increase of the To take a concrete case:—Assume that the tenant's contributiog to the tax as ascertained under the Act is—say—one fourth. §) long as the total amount of the land tax, which I will assumeis £30, does not vary, the tenant's contribution remains at £7 10s.anl_ the incidence of this burden is not altered by a covenant to pay an additional rent bearing a fixed proportion to the fixed sum a £30, which, in the case of a stipulation such as that in the present case, will be £10. text: Suppose then that the land tax is doubled, and becomes £60. 'The result of the stipulation for additional rent, read as cumulative upon, and not substitutional for, that provided by the Act, would therefore be to effect an alteration in the proportional contribution of the tenant, as at the date of the lease. text: In regard to the amount claimed by th appellant as payable in 1902, sec. 63 of the Land and Ineomp them was the Statute as searching in its terms as sec. 63 of th Land and Income Tax Assessment Act 1895. That se aims directly at every contract agreement or understanding which has or purports to have, or which might have, the effect of in any way affecting the incidence of any tax payable under the A The "incidence" of the tax must mean the burden of the tax, both as regards the person upon whom it falls in the first instane, the contribution which he is authorized to obtain from others, an the proportion in which the burden of the tax falls upon the original taxpayer and upon the contributor. One illustration will be suficient to show that" incidence " of the tax cannot be restricted tothe narrow meaning of " payment of the tax directly to the Commis sioners." A direct guarantee by a tenant to repay the landlord the amount of tax paid by him to the Commissioners as provided by the Act would certainly not affect the incidence of the tax in that matrow sense, because the landlord would in that case still remainthe taxpayer; yet no one could say that such an agreement would not be contrary to law if the section is to have any practical operation.

text: Tn either case the tenant will in effect bear the burden of a proportion of the tax different from that which the Statute has directed he shall bear. I may add that I entirely concur in the reasoning by which my learned brother the Chief Justice has arrived at the same conclusion. Under the cireumstances it is in my opinion plain that this agreement has, to use the words of the Statute, "the effect of affecting the incidence of the tax" and that therefore it is void within the meaning of sec. 63. 'The appellant is therefore not entitled to recover the amount claimed in respect of 1902.

text: Coming now to the amount sued for as payable in 1903, we must consider the terms of the Land Taw (Leases) Act 1902 — This Act applies only to leases where the term is not less than thirty years. In regard to these leases it takes the place of the Land and Income Tax Assessment Act 1895. From tie beginning of 1903 the land tax in respect of such leases is raised and made payable by this special Act alone.
citation: Harris v Sydney Glass and Tile Co [1904] HCA 47

text: By sec. 4 landlord and tenant are both made taxpayers, but in such proportions as may be equitably adjusted by the Commissioners of Taxation in proportion to the value of their respective interests. Sec. 5 enacts that the incidence of the tax so provided for shall not be affected by any agreement. It does not make void any agreement affecting the incidence of the tax as sec. 63 of the earlier Act does in regard to agreements contrary to the Act of 1895; it merely declares that the agreement shall not affect the incidence of the tax.
citation: Harris v Sydney Glass and Tile Co [1904] HCA 47

text: If the agreement now under consideration had been made after the passing of this Act it would not be void. It could not relieve the tenant from the obligation to pay his proportion of the tax to the Commissioners, but he would be just as liable under it to repay to the landlord the agreed proportion of the tax as he would be to perform any other agreement. 'The agreement however was made, as we know, before the passing of the Act of 1902, and became, for certain purposes, void on its execution. The question now arises, how can the landlorl Met under this Act by virtue of an agreement made at a a8 yo, ryhen the rights of the parties were regulated by the Act of Hamas yand which, as I have pointed out, was void in so far as it See ion of that Act? For the purpose of answering Grass axp Tite Co. nis question it becomes necessary to look again at sec. 63 of the of 1895, It will be observed that that section does not make void the whole agreement contravening its provisions.
citation: Harris v Sydney Glass and Tile Co [1904] HCA 47

text: It uses expression "shall be wholly void and inoperative '9 fur assuch agreement . . . might have the effect aforethat is the "effect of in any way affecting the incidence of O'Connor J. tax." 'The section then goes on, " without prejudice to the ity of such agreement in any other respect or for any other se." The only reasonable interpretation of those words is, ny opinion, this—that such part of the agreement as has the bet of affecting the incidence of the tax becomes wholly void. 'Mhis interpretation is not only reasonable but in accordance with ny grammatical construction. Any other reading of the en acs to the absurdity, pointed out by the Chief Justice, of agreement void and dead under the existing law, but capable of 'ining new life and validity from some future alteration of the lay, which will render lawful that which was unlawful. Having mand to the object of the legislature apparent on the face of see. the Court is bound to avoid an interpretation leading to such an thsurd consequence, if any other interpretation is grammatically sible Taking the view of see. 63 that I have explained, I am opinion that the part of the agreement which violated the pro§ of that section was void from its inception, and never had _ tty validity for any purpose, and therefore cannot now be made tfietual for the purpose of making good the appellant's claim mnespect of the amount alleged to be due in 1903. For these sI concur with my learned brothers in holding that: the n of the covenant in question is void in respect of the 'ppellant's claim for both 1902 and 1903, and that the decision of tleSupreme Court on this part of the case must be upheld.
